Here is where I was trying to go with this: Think of it as our Bamboo OSX adapter on top of `build-and-test-nupic-bindings.sh`, so nothing outside should ever need to pass `--user` to this script. I.e., making the OSX build using this script in our Bamboo environment won't work without `--user`, so it shouldn't be a command-line arg at all. To add to this, one "major" change is the [removal of mapping types](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/6.2/removal-of-types.html). The norm now is to set the `doc_type` for any mapping to always be  `_doc` (so [`ad-v1.0.0`](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/blob/master/invenio_marc21/mappings/v5/marc21/authority/ad-v1.0.0.json#L7) should be just `_doc`).

This doesn't really affect us since all of our mappings have only one document type... We might have to check out how this affects `elasticsearch-py` since the `doc_type` paremter for calling e.g. `current_search.index(index='records', doc_type=..., ...)` might be different or simpllified.

Furthermore check for the contents of the eprints children, like:
```
content_children = children[0].getchildren()
assert len(content_children) == 1
assert content_children[0].tag == '{http://www.openarchives.org/OAI/2.0/eprints}URL'
assert content_children[0].text == 'http://arXiv.org/arXiv_content.htm'
(...)
metaPoli_children = children[1].getchildren()
assert len(metaPoli_children) == 2
...
```

I was thinking about this a little more. One side effect of the `display.HTML` is that if the underlying dataset changes, the next time someone loads this notebook, they'll get a different image. That's not a good thing if someone is telling a story with a dataset and making changes to it along the way as part of an analysis. To avoid this issue, we should capture a snapshot of the image and have it embedded in the notebook. We can do that with this:

```python
return IPython.display.Image(url=static_url, embed=True, format='png')
```

OK here is the problem in step by step detail.

I want to be able to run the following on all IGangaFile objects to get my file at some destination:
```
inputFile.localDir = tmpDir
inputFile.get()
```

I expect from an API that I get __ALL__ of the files in the tmpDir.

This will __NOT__ work with `LocalFile` due to the fact that `localDir` is used to point to the file in the first place.

Adding an exception for the `LocalFile` is against the whole point of this PR and means that we have some _funny_ behaviour with one of the objects which all share this same interface which just confuses me let alone future developers.

Using:
```
inputFile.get(localPath=tmpDir)
```

Allows the file regardless of the type to transfer a file here.
Allowing an optional parameter doesn't effect the default behaviour of the `get()` method being called and simply adds extra functionality.

A reasonable argument is that the file object should then have it's `localDir` replaced with the contents of `tmpdir`. In the case of `LocalFile` this represents a move command which is potentially dangerous so we shouldn't delete a user's file on them which raises the question of what is proper here?

Another possible solution is:

```
inputFile.transfer(localPath=tmpDir)
```

Which allows the `get()` functionality to be untouched but still leaves us with a question of which file is the LocalFile at this point?
Obviously different developers have valid opinions on this but ultimately the user pointed us to 1 file which we're copying for them.
We either lose information about 1 of the locations or expand the schema to allow this file to have multiple locations on disk which seems counter-intuitive.


Another solution is to yes change the `LocalFile` schema so that `localDir` is always only the place where the file will end up after `get()` and will be before `put()`.
We should really be using something like `location` for the construction of `LocalFile` in order to do this but this is an invasive possibly completely incompatible change.

There is also no way to distinguish a function that returns nothing from the error. That's a dangerous assumption to have. I was wondering if this check is needed.

At the moment it seems the only place that it is used is in `rpc/client.py` in all the locations like this:

https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/network/rpc/client.py#L1563-1570

So the form the check takes is:

1. trying the call (only for something that does return a value - for other things this check won't work) , 
2. if it's empty string then check if the address has code, if not then we give the no code in address error
3. If the address has code and we get this then only other possible things that happened are:
     i. The contract does not have the function we try to call
     ii. The function had an exception in the outermost call
     iii. The function returns nothing.

Hmm now that I write the above down, I see that even if we change the order of checks we gain nothing and even though I still think it's a very flaky thing to rely on as it relies on the type of function calls and the behaviour of the RPC call it's the best we can now do.

So I will bring back the test, to detect when/if this assumption is broken by a client update.
